/.title: Neural Network
/.content:
Hello, and welcome to my neural network! This is the place where I upload the notes I've taken and compiled from my Mathematics and Computer Science degree at [UQ](https://uq.edu.au/).

The neural network functions very similarly to [Obsidian](https://obsidian.md/);  features like page backlinks, tags, and interactive graphs are supported. You can also full-text search around the neural network with `Ctrl + k`.

## Courses
Below is a list of all the courses that I have taken thus far. Courses that I'm currently taking are **bolded**. If I've taken my own notes for a course, a link to its tag in the neural network is attached.

/notes/MATH1051/1-eulers-formula.title: Euler's Formula
/notes/MATH1051/1-eulers-formula.content:
*Euler's formula* states that for any real number $x$,

$$e^{ix} = \cos x + i \sin x.$$

When $x = \pi$, Euler's formula can be written in the form $e^{\pi i} + 1 = 0$, which is known as *Euler's identity*.

/notes/MATH1051/2-function-composition.title: Function Composition
/notes/MATH1051/2-function-composition.content:
Let $f: X \to Y$ and $g: Y \to Z$ be two [[notes/MATH1051/2 functions|functions]]. The *composition* of $f$ and $g$, denoted $f \circ g: X \to Z$, is defined by

$$(f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x)).$$

Function composition is generally non-commutative; that is, $f \circ g \neq g \circ f$.

/notes/MATH1051/2-functions.title: Functions
/notes/MATH1051/2-functions.content:
A *function* $f$ from a set $X$ to a set $Y$, denoted $f: X \to Y$, assigns each element $x \in X$ exactly one element $f(x) \in Y$. The set $X$ is the *domain* of $f$ and the set $Y$ is the *codomain* of $f$.

The *image* or *range* of $f$, that is $f(X)$, is a subset of $Y$. The range is not necessarily equal to the codomain.

Geometrically, a function satisfies the vertical line test (any constant vertical line cuts through the function at most once).

/notes/MATH1051/2-injective-functions.title: Injective Functions
/notes/MATH1051/2-injective-functions.content:
A [[notes/MATH1051/2 functions|function]] $f: X \to Y$ is said to be *injective* or *one-to-one* if, for all $x_1, x_2 \in X$, we have $f(x_1) = f(x_2) \implies x_1 = x_2$.

Geometrically, a function is injective if it satisfies the horizontal line test (any constant horizontal line cuts through the function at most once).

/notes/MATH1051/2-inverse-functions.title: Inverse Functions
/notes/MATH1051/2-inverse-functions.content: Let $f: X \to Y$ be a bijective ([[notes/MATH1051/2 injective functions|injective]] and surjective) function. The *inverse function* $f^{-1}: Y \to X$ is defined by $f^{-1}(y) = x$ for each $y \in Y$ and $x \in X$. We have $f^{-1}(y) = x \iff y = f(x)$.

/notes/MATH1051/3-limit-of-a-sequence.title: Limit of a Sequence
/notes/MATH1051/3-limit-of-a-sequence.content:
Let $\{a_n\}_{n=0}^\infty$ be a [[notes/MATH1051/3 sequences|sequence]]. If $a_n$ approaches some number $\mathscr{l} \in \mathbb{R}$ as $n$ gets larger and larger, we write

$$\lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = \mathscr{l}.$$

If a sequence $\{a_n\}$ has limit $\mathscr{l} \in \mathbb{R}$, we say that $a_n$ *converges* to $\mathscr{l}$, and that the sequence is *convergent*. Otherwise, $a_n$ *diverges* and the sequence is *divergent*.

/notes/MATH1051/3-sequences.title: Sequences
/notes/MATH1051/3-sequences.content: A *sequence* $\{a_n\}$ is an ordered list of numbers. Formally, a sequence can be defined as a [[notes/MATH1051/2 functions|function]] from the natural numbers $\mathbb{N}$ to the elements at each point in the sequence.

/notes/MATH1051/3-squeeze-theorem.title: Squeeze Theorem
/notes/MATH1051/3-squeeze-theorem.content:
Let $a_n, b_n, c_n$ be [[notes/MATH1051/3 sequences|sequences]] such that, for some $N \in \mathbb{N}$, we have $a_n \leq b_n \leq c_n$ for all $n \geq N$ and $\displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} c_n = \mathscr{l}$. Then

$$\lim_{n\to\infty} b_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = \lim_{n\to\infty} b_n = \mathscr{l}.$$
